1: wayyaʿăśû bənê yiśrāʾēl hāraʿ bəʿênê YHWH wayyittənēm YHWH bəyaḏ-miḏyān šeḇaʿ šānîm — 'The sons of Israel did the evil in the eyes of YHWH, and YHWH gave them into the hand of Midian seven years.' The cycle formula now has seven years of oppression — the number seven intensifies the judgment. Midian (miḏyān) was Israel's ancestral ally (Jethro/Reuel was Moses's father-in-law) turned adversary; the oppression is thus a family betrayal as well as a theological judgment. The repetition of nātan bəyāḏ (H5414 H3027 — gave into the hand) shows YHWH as the active agent of punishment, not merely the passive permitter.
6: wayyiddal yiśrāʾēl məʾōd mippənê miḏyān — 'Israel was made very weak/low by reason of Midian.' dālal (H1809, to dangle/hang low, to be brought low — used of exhausted, depleted persons) captures the totality of the decline: Israel has been reduced to hiding in mountain caves (v2), and the Midianites swarm the land like locusts (ʾarḇeh H697, v5) consuming everything. wayyiṣʿaqû bənê-yiśrāʾēl ʾel-YHWH — Israel's cry to YHWH here is preceded by the divine response of a prophet (vv7-10) who diagnoses the problem before YHWH sends the deliverer. The prophetic word precedes the gift of a judge — the standard means of grace is the word.
11: wayyāḇōʾ malʾak YHWH wayyēšeḇ taḥat hāʾēlāh ʾăšer biʿoprāh — 'The angel of YHWH came and sat under the oak that was at Ophrah.' malʾak YHWH (H4397 H3068, messenger/angel of YHWH) — in the patriarchal and early period narratives, the malʾak YHWH is a figure who speaks as YHWH in the first person, is identified with YHWH (v14: 'YHWH looked at him'), yet is distinguishable from him. This figure is the pre-incarnate Son in many patristic readings (Justin Martyr, Irenaeus). wayyāšaḇ (H3427, sat/dwelt) — the angel's posture is that of one settling in to wait, not passing through. ʾēlāh (H424, terebinth/oak) is the sacred tree type under which theophanies and divine encounters often occur (cf. Gen 12:6; Judg 4:11).
12: wayyērāʾ ʾēlāyw malʾak YHWH wayyōʾmer ʾēlāyw YHWH ʿimmāk gibbôr heḥāyil — 'The angel of YHWH appeared to him and said to him: YHWH is with you, O mighty warrior.' The greeting YHWH ʿimmāk (H3068 H5973 = YHWH is with you) is the covenant presence formula — the same used with Isaac (Gen 26:24), Moses (Exod 3:12), Joshua (Josh 1:5), and later with the virgin Mary (Luke 1:28 — kúrios metà soû). gibbôr heḥāyil (H1368 H2428, mighty man of valor/warrior of strength) — addressed to a man threshing wheat in secret to hide it from Midian — is not a description of Gideon's present state but a declaration of what YHWH sees and is calling him to be. Divine calling names a destiny, not a present reality.
13: wayyōʾmer ʾēlāyw giḏʿôn bî ʾăḏōnî — 'Gideon said to him: Please, my lord...' Gideon's response is a theodicy question: if YHWH is with us, why has all this happened? ʾayyēh kol-nipləʾōṯāyw — 'where are all his wonderful deeds?' niplāʾōṯ (H6381 root, wonders/miracles) — Gideon appeals to the Exodus tradition: the fathers told us YHWH brought us up from Egypt. The contrast between past miracle and present disaster is the classic lament structure: we remember your acts of old, but now you have abandoned us. The question is honest and is not rebuked — YHWH answers it not with explanation but with commissioning.
14: wayyipeŋ ʾēlāyw YHWH wayyōʾmer lēk bəkōḥăkā zeh wəhôšaʿtā ʾet-yiśrāʾēl — 'YHWH turned to him and said: Go in this might of yours and save Israel.' pānāh ʾēl (H6437, to turn/face toward) — YHWH turns attention directly to Gideon, a gesture of focused divine address. The phrase bəkōḥăkā zeh (in this your might/strength) is ironic given Gideon's expressed weakness — but 'this' may refer to the audacity of his theodicy question, which is itself a form of faith: you know the exodus tradition and you are holding YHWH to it. hălōʾ šəlaḥtîkā — 'Have I not sent you?' — the commissioning formula that echoes Moses in Exod 3:12.
15: wayyōʾmer ʾēlāyw bî ʾăḏōnî bammāh ʾôšîaʿ ʾet-yiśrāʾēl hinnēh ʾalpî haddal bimənašše wəʾānōkî haqqāṭōn bəḇêt ʾāḇî — 'He said to him: Please, my lord, how shall I save Israel? Behold, my clan is the weakest (haddal) in Manasseh, and I am the least (haqqāṭōn) in my father's house.' dallāh/haddal (H1800/1803, the weak/lowly/thin/impoverished one) and qāṭōn (H6996, small/least/youngest) frame Gideon's objection in terms of social insignificance. The double diminutive — weakest tribe, youngest in family — establishes the principle that YHWH's deliverer will carry no natural advantage. Compare Moses's objections (Exod 4:10: 'I am not a man of words'), Jeremiah's (Jer 1:6: 'I am only a youth'), and Paul's (1 Cor 2:3: 'in weakness and in fear and in much trembling').
16: wayyōʾmer ʾēlāyw YHWH kî ʾehyeh ʿimmāk wəhikkîtā ʾet-miḏyān kəʾîš ʾeḥāḏ — 'YHWH said to him: But I will be with you, and you shall strike Midian as one man.' kî ʾehyeh ʿimmāk — the divine answer to human inadequacy is always the same: 'I will be with you' (ʾehyeh = H1961, the imperfect of hāyāh to be, related to the divine name YHWH revealed in Exod 3:14). The commissioning formula here exactly matches Exod 3:12: God's answer to Moses's 'Who am I?' was 'I will be with you' — not a credential for Moses but a promise of divine presence. The victory will be so complete that Midian will fall 'as one man' (kəʾîš ʾeḥāḏ) — a singular unified defeat.
22: wayyarʾ giḏʿôn kî-malʾak YHWH hûʾ — 'Gideon saw that he was the angel of YHWH.' Only after the offering is consumed by fire and the angel vanishes does Gideon recognize what he had been speaking to. wayyōʾmer ʾahāh ʾăḏōnāy YHWH kî-ʿal-kēn rāʾîtî malʾak YHWH pānîm ʾel-pānîm — 'He said: Alas, Lord YHWH! For I have seen the angel of YHWH face to face!' pānîm ʾel-pānîm (H6440, face to face) — the same idiom as Moses and YHWH (Exod 33:11; Deut 34:10). The terror is that seeing YHWH's face brings death (Exod 33:20; Gen 32:30 — Jacob 'I have seen God face to face and yet my life was delivered'). The ancient belief that direct divine encounter is lethal makes Gideon's fear theologically coherent.
23: wayyōʾmer lô YHWH šālôm lāk ʾal-tîrāʾ lōʾ tāmût — 'YHWH said to him: Peace be to you. Do not fear; you will not die.' The divine word counters the panic with šālôm (H7965, peace/wholeness/well-being). This direct divine assurance — 'you will not die' — is not merely comfort but promise: Gideon's mission is not yet complete. The assurance becomes the content of the altar name.
24: wayyiḇen šām giḏʿôn mizbēaḥ laYHWH wayyiqrāʾ-lô YHWH šālôm — 'Gideon built an altar there to YHWH and called it YHWH-Shalom.' mizbēaḥ (H4196, altar — from zāḇaḥ to slaughter/sacrifice) built at a theophany site is a patriarchal pattern (Abraham, Gen 12:7-8; 13:18; Jacob, Gen 33:20). The altar name YHWH Šālôm (H3068 H7965) — YHWH is Peace — encodes the content of the divine assurance at the encounter. Divine names derived from encounters (El-Roi, Gen 16:14; El-Shaddai; YHWH-Jireh, Gen 22:14) are theology inscribed in geography: future worshipers at this altar invoke the specific aspect of YHWH revealed here.
25: wayhî ballaylāh hahûʾ wayyōʾmer lô YHWH qaḥ ʾet-par haššôr — 'That same night YHWH said to him: Take your father's bull...' The command is a test of Gideon's covenant loyalty: he must first destroy his own family's Baal altar and Asherah pole before leading Israel against Midian. mizbəaḥ habbāʿal ʾăšer ləʾāḇîkā — 'the Baal altar that belongs to your father' — YHWH's first demand is internal house-cleaning. The Baal altar in Joash's household (Gideon's father) represents the syncretism that caused Israel's oppression in the first place. ʾăšērāh (H842, Asherah — the Canaanite fertility goddess, goddess of the sea, companion of Baal) represented as a sacred pole or stylized tree — the standard companion-object to Baal altars.
34: wərûaḥ YHWH lāḇəšāh ʾet-giḏʿôn wayyitqaʿ baššôpār — 'The Spirit of YHWH clothed Gideon, and he blew the trumpet.' lāḇaš (H3847, to clothe/put on) — the Spirit 'clothing' Gideon is a striking anthropomorphism: Gideon becomes the garment that the Spirit wears, or alternatively, the Spirit enrobes Gideon. The metaphor of Spirit-clothing appears for Amasai (1 Chr 12:18) and Zechariah (2 Chr 24:20). The normal phrase is the Spirit 'coming upon' or 'rushing upon' (ṣālaḥ) someone — the clothing metaphor suggests sustained empowerment for a sustained campaign. The šôpār (H7782, ram's horn trumpet) is the battle signal and the covenant assembly instrument (Exod 19:16; Josh 6:4-5).
36: wayyōʾmer giḏʿôn ʾel-hāʾĕlōhîm ʾim-yešəkā môšîaʿ bəyāḏî ʾet-yiśrāʾēl kaʾăšer dibbartā — 'Gideon said to God: If you will save Israel by my hand, as you have spoken...' The fleece test is often cited as exemplary of seeking a sign from God, but the text's tone is ambiguous. Gideon explicitly acknowledges YHWH's promise ('as you have spoken') — the sign is not a demand for proof before obedience but a request for confirmation after commission. gizzāh (H1492, fleece — from gāzaz to shear) — wool as a medium for dew/water is physically apt; wool retains moisture exceptionally well. The reversal in vv37-40 (dry fleece, wet ground; then wet fleece, dry ground) moves beyond natural wool-absorption, making the sign unambiguously supernatural.
40: wayyaʿaś ʾĕlōhîm kēn ballaylāh hahûʾ — 'God did so that night.' The divine compliance with the second fleece test is notable: YHWH accommodates Gideon's persistent seeking. The phrase wayyaʿas ʾĕlōhîm (God did/acted) is terse and unqualified — no rebuke for asking twice, no reluctance expressed. The pattern of double-testing (dry/wet → wet/dry) ensures that the first result cannot be explained naturally, and its reversal confirms the first as intentional. Gideon's persistent seeking will be vindicated: the man who needed two fleece confirmations will later need only a single overheard dream (7:13-15) to go into battle immediately.
2: watteʿaz yaḏ-miḏyān ʿal-yiśrāʾēl mippənê miḏyān ʿāśû lāhem bənê yiśrāʾēl ʾet-hamminharôt ʾăšer behehārîm wəʾet-hamməʿārôt wəʾet-hammǝṣāḏôt — 'The hand of Midian overpowered Israel, and because of Midian the sons of Israel made themselves the dens in the mountains and the caves and the strongholds.' ʿāzaz (H5810, to be strong/prevail) in the Qal describes Midian's military dominance as a physical weight pressing down on Israel. The three hiding places — minharôt (clefts/shafts H4492), məʿārôt (caves H4631), məṣāḏôt (strongholds H4679) — describe the progressive retreat of a society from its land into the earth itself. The agrarian people have become cave-dwellers: a complete reversal of the Exodus gift of a land flowing with milk and honey.
3: wəhāyāh ʾim-zāraʿ yiśrāʾēl wəʿālāh miḏyān wəʿămālēq ûḇənê-qeḏem wəʿālû ʿālāyw — 'Whenever Israel planted, Midian and Amalek and the people of the East would come up against them.' zāraʿ (H2232, to sow/plant) is the fundamental act of settled agrarian life; the moment of planting triggers the Midianite raid. The three-part coalition — Midian, Amalek, and the people of the East — describes a pan-desert alliance of pastoral/nomadic peoples preying systematically on Israel's agricultural surplus. The timing is deliberate: they do not prevent planting but destroy the harvest, maximizing Israel's labor and their own gain.
4: wayyaḥănû ʿălêhem wayyašḥîtû ʾet-yəḇûl hāʾāreṣ ʿaḏ-bōʾăkā ʿazzāh wəlōʾ-yišʾîrû mihyāh bəyiśrāʾēl wəlōʾ-śeh wəlōʾ-šôr wəlōʾ-ḥamôr — 'They would encamp against Israel and destroy the produce of the land as far as Gaza, and leave no sustenance for Israel, and no sheep or ox or donkey.' šāḥat (H7843, to destroy/corrupt/ruin) — the verb used of the Flood (Gen 6:12) and Sodom (Gen 19:13); the Midianite destruction of crops is described in the most extreme terms. The enumeration of no sheep, no ox, no donkey is the destruction of the entire domestic economy. Israel is being systematically stripped of its covenant inheritance.
5: kî hem ûmiqdnêhem yaʿălû ûʾohŏlêhem kāʾarḇeh lārōḇ ûlāhem ûligmallêhem ʾên mispar — 'For they and their livestock would come up with their tents, coming like locusts for number; and they and their camels were without number.' ʾarḇeh (H697, locust — the great plague insect of Exod 10) — the locust comparison is the most devastating available in Hebrew rhetoric: a swarm that consumes everything and cannot be stopped by conventional means. The emphasis on the innumerability of camels is historically significant: the Midianite raids represent the first recorded large-scale military use of camels in the ancient Near East, a new technology of desert mobility that gave raiders a reach no foot-army could counter.
7: wayhî kî-ṣāʿaqû bənê-yiśrāʾēl ʾel-YHWH ʿal-ʾōḏôt miḏyān — 'When the sons of Israel cried out to YHWH on account of Midian, YHWH sent a prophet to them.' The sequence — Israel cries out, YHWH responds — is the judge cycle's heartbeat. But here, instead of sending a judge, YHWH first sends a prophet. šālaḥ nāḇîʾ (H7971 H5030, sent a prophet) — the unnamed prophet (vv8-10) is unique in Judges: an anonymous prophetic voice that delivers a covenant lawsuit before the theophanic commissioning of Gideon. The word must precede the deliverer: diagnosis before remedy.
8: wayyōʾmer lāhem kōh-ʾāmar YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl ʾānōkî heʿēlêtî ʾetkem mimmiṣrayim — 'He said to them: Thus says YHWH the God of Israel: I brought you up from Egypt.' kōh ʾāmar YHWH — 'Thus says YHWH' — the prophetic messenger formula; the anonymous prophet speaks with full prophetic authority. The content is a recitation of the saving history: Exodus from Egypt, deliverance from all who oppressed (v9), gift of the land. The historical recitation is the covenant lawsuit's first act — establishing what YHWH has done, which makes Israel's apostasy inexcusable. miyyaḏ kol-lōḥăṣêkem (from the hand of all your oppressors) — the summary of covenant protection history.
9: waʾăṣîl ʾetkem miyyaḏ miṣrayim ûmiyyaḏ kol-lōḥăṣêkem wāʾăgārēš ʾôtām mippənêkem wāʾettənāh lākem ʾet-ʾarṣām — 'I delivered you from the hand of Egypt and from the hand of all who oppressed you; I drove them out before you and gave you their land.' Three sovereign acts: nāṣal (H5337, to deliver/rescue from), gāraš (H1644, to drive out/expel), nātan (H5414, to give). The succession of divine first-person verbs is the covenant-establishing narrative in miniature: rescue, dispossession of enemies, gift of land. The land now being ravaged by Midian was given by YHWH — its violation is thus a theological insult to the Giver.
10: wāʾōmar lākem ʾănî YHWH ʾĕlōhêkem lōʾ tîrəʾû ʾet-ʾĕlōhê hāʾĕmōrî ʾăšer ʾattem yôšəḇîm bəʾarṣām wəlōʾ šəmaʿtem bəqôlî — 'I said to you: I am YHWH your God; you shall not fear the gods of the Amorites in whose land you dwell. But you have not obeyed my voice.' The covenant charge is delivered with the first commandment formula: ʾănî YHWH ʾĕlōhêkem — 'I am YHWH your God' (Exod 20:2). The prohibition is against fearing (yārēʾ H3372 — the term encompasses both religious reverence and practical submission) the gods of the Amorites. wəlōʾ šəmaʿtem bəqôlî — 'but you have not obeyed my voice' — the covenant indictment in two Hebrew words. No further rebuke is given; the prophet states the case and stops. YHWH's grace is that he then sends a deliverer anyway.
17: wayyōʾmer ʾēlāyw ʾim-nāʾ māṣāʾtî ḥēn bəʿênêkā wəʿāśîtā lî ʾôt ʾattāh mədabbēr ʿimmî — 'He said to him: If I have found favor in your eyes, give me a sign that it is you who speaks with me.' māṣāʾ ḥēn bəʿênê (H4672 H2580 H5869, to find grace/favor in the eyes of) — the covenant petition formula, used by Moses before YHWH (Exod 33:13, 16) and by Gideon here. The request for a sign (ʾôt H226) is not unbelief but the prophetic/covenantal procedure for verification: Gideon is following the Deuteronomic principle (Deut 18:21-22) that a prophet's word requires confirmation. The sign requested is not a cosmic event but a personal demonstration: remain here while I prepare an offering.
18: ʾal-nāʾ tāmûš mizzeh ʿaḏ-bōʾî ʾēlêkā wəhôṣēʾtî ʾet-minḥātî wəhinnáḥtî ləpānêkā — 'Please do not depart from here until I come to you and bring out my offering and set it before you.' sûr/mûš (H4185, to depart/move away) — Gideon's plea that the angel not leave echoes the covenant petition for divine presence; without the angel's continued presence, the sign cannot be given. minḥāh (H4503, offering/gift — the grain offering, but here a hospitality gift that becomes a sacrificial offering) — Gideon prepares what will become the content of the sign. The angel's 'I will wait until you return' (ʾēšēb ʿaḏ-šûḇekā) is the divine patience with human hesitation.
19: wəgiḏʿôn bāʾ wayaʿaś gəḏî-ʿizzîm wəʾêpāh qemeaḥ maṣṣôt — 'Gideon went in and prepared a young goat and unleavened cakes from an ephah of flour.' The offering is substantial: a young goat (gəḏî ʿizzîm H1423 H5795) and an entire ephah (approximately 22 liters) of flour made into unleavened cakes (maṣṣôt H4682 — the same bread of the Passover/Exodus). maṣṣôt is not merely economical (it can be prepared quickly) but theologically freighted: unleavened bread is the bread of hasty divine action. Gideon's offering takes on the character of a Passover meal — and the angel's fire will consume it as an altar-sacrifice.
20: wayyōʾmer ʾēlāyw malʾak hāʾĕlōhîm qaḥ ʾet-habbāśār wəʾet-hammaṣṣôt wəhannáḥ ʾel-hassellaʿ hazzeh wəhašpôk ʾet-hammārāq — 'The angel of God said to him: Take the meat and the unleavened cakes, and put them on this rock, and pour the broth over them.' The rock (sellaʿ H5553, a large crag/boulder) becomes the altar; the angel's instruction converts hospitality into sacrifice. wayyaʿaś kēn — he did so. The angel's redirection of the meal from table to rock is the pivotal moment: the guest is revealed as the host of a divine encounter, not the recipient of human hospitality.
21: wayyišlaḥ malʾak YHWH ʾet-qəṣēh hammišʿenet ʾăšer bəyāḏô wayyigaʿ baббāśār ûḇammaṣṣôt wayyaʿal hāʾēš min-hassellaʿ wattiʾkal ʾet-habbāśār wəʾet-hammaṣṣôt ûmalʾak YHWH hālak mēʿênāyw — 'The angel of YHWH reached out the tip of the staff that was in his hand and touched the meat and the unleavened cakes. Fire sprang up from the rock and consumed the meat and the unleavened cakes. And the angel of YHWH vanished from his sight.' The fire from the rock consuming the offering is the theophanic fire-sign: the same pattern as Elijah's altar on Carmel (1 Kgs 18:38 — 'fire of YHWH fell and consumed'), Manoah's offering (13:20 — 'the flame went up toward heaven from the altar'). The immediate disappearance of the angel completes the revelation: this was no human messenger but the divine presence itself.
26: ûḇānîtā mizbēaḥ laYHWH ʾĕlōhêkā ʿal-rōʾš hamāʿôz hazzeh bammāʿărāḵāh — 'Build an altar to YHWH your God on top of this stronghold, with the stones laid in order.' māʿôz (H4581, stronghold/refuge/fortress) — the altar is to be built on the highest point of the fortified site, the place of maximum visibility. hammāʿărāḵāh (H4634, in proper order/arrangement) — altar-building has a prescribed form; the stones must be arranged correctly, contrasting with the haphazard idolatry of the Baal altar below. The second bull (seven years old, corresponding to the seven years of Midian's oppression, v25) is to be sacrificed using the wood of the Asherah pole — the instrument of idolatry becomes the fuel for the legitimate sacrifice.
27: wayyiqqaḥ giḏʿôn ʿăśārāh ʾănāšîm mēʿăḇāḏāyw wayyaʿaś kaʾăšer dibbēr ʾēlāyw YHWH — 'Gideon took ten of his servants and did just as YHWH had told him.' Obedience despite fear (wayyîrāʾ ʾet-bêt ʾāḇîw wəʾet-ʾanšê hāʿîr — he feared his father's household and the men of the city) is still counted as obedience. The nighttime action is not a failure of courage but a prudent reading of context — Gideon acts when action is possible. His obedience 'by night' mirrors the Exodus pattern: YHWH struck Egypt at midnight (Exod 12:29); the decisive acts of liberation often occur in darkness.
28: wayyaškîmû ʾanšê hāʿîr babbōqer wəhinnēh nuttaṣ mizbəaḥ habbāʿal wəhāʾăšērāh ʾăšer ʿālāyw kurrāṯāh — 'When the men of the city rose early in the morning, behold, the altar of Baal was torn down (nāṯaṣ), and the Asherah beside it was cut down.' nāṯaṣ (H5422, to tear down/demolish — the same verb used in Deut 7:5 for the commanded destruction of Canaanite altars) — Gideon has obeyed the Deuteronomic altar law. The use of the expected covenant verb confirms that Gideon's action was theologically correct. The Asherah 'cut down' (kārat H3772, to cut off — covenant language used for both cutting covenants and cutting off those who break them) — the religious infrastructure of apostasy has been removed.
29: wayyōʾmərû ʾîš ʾel-rēʿēhû mî ʿāśāh haddāḇār hazzeh wayyidərəšû wayyəḇaqqēšû wayyōʾmərû giḏʿôn ben-yôʾāš ʿāśāh haddāḇār hazzeh — 'They said to one another: Who has done this thing? And when they searched and inquired, they said: Gideon the son of Joash has done this thing.' dāraš (H1875, to seek/inquire/investigate) and bāqaš (H1245, to seek/search) — the community's investigation uses judicial inquiry language: they seek to identify the perpetrator. The identification of Gideon triggers the demand for his death (v30) — destroying an idol is a capital offense in the community that worships it. The confrontation sets up Joash's decisive response.
30: wayyōʾmərû ʾanšê hāʿîr ʾel-yôʾāš hôṣēʾ ʾet-binkā wəyāmōt kî nāṯaṣ ʾet-mizbəaḥ habbāʿal wəkî kārat hāʾăšērāh ʾăšer ʿālāyw — 'The men of the city said to Joash: Bring out your son so that he may die, for he has torn down the altar of Baal and cut down the Asherah beside it.' The death-demand (wəyāmōt) from Israelites defending a Baal altar is the measure of how far Israel has fallen: the covenant people are now prepared to execute one of their own for obeying the covenant law. The demand is made to Joash as head of household — tribal justice operated through family responsibility.
31: wayyōʾmer yôʾāš ləkol ʾăšer ʿāmḏû ʿālāyw hăʾattem tərîḇûn laббāʿal — 'Joash said to all who stood against him: Will you contend for Baal? Will you save him?' Joash's rebuttal is theologically sharp: if Baal is a god, he can defend himself. rîḇ (H7378, to contend/plead a legal case) — Joash reframes the confrontation as a legal case: Baal can be his own plaintiff if his altar has been wronged. ʾăšer yārîḇ lô yûmāt ʿaḏ-habbōqer — 'Whoever contends for him shall be put to death by morning' — a rhetorical reversal: the crowd that came to execute Gideon is now threatened with the same fate if they persist. Joash's argument will later be the standard for prophetic polemic against idols (cf. Isa 44:9-20).
32: wayyiqrāʾ-lô bayyôm hahûʾ yərubbaʿal lēʾmōr yāreb-bô habbāʿal kî nāṯaṣ ʾet-mizbəḥô — 'Therefore on that day Gideon was called Yerubbaal, saying: Let Baal contend against him, because he tore down his altar.' yərubbaʿal (H3378, Yerubba'al — Let Baal contend/plead) — the name is a satirical monument: it commemorates Baal's inability to defend himself. Names from encounters crystallize the theological content of the event; this name crystallizes Baal's impotence. nāṯaṣ (he tore down) — the name will follow Gideon throughout the Gideon-Abimelech narrative as an ironic commentary: the Baal-contender is the one YHWH uses to defeat Midian.
33: wəkol-miḏyān wəʿămālēq ûḇənê-qeḏem neʾespû yaḥdāw wayyaʿăḇərû wayyaḥănû bəʿēmeq yizrəʿēl — 'Then all the Midianites and the Amalekites and the people of the East assembled, crossed the Jordan, and encamped in the Valley of Jezreel.' The gathering of the coalition in the Jezreel Valley is the military situation that requires Gideon's response. ʾāsap (H622, to gather/assemble) and ʿābar (H5674, to cross over — the Jordan crossing that mirrors the Exodus in reverse: enemies coming in to the land) set the scene for the climactic confrontation of chapters 7-8.
35: wayyišlaḥ malʾāḵîm bəkol-mənašše wayyizzāʿēq gam-hûʾ ʾaḥărāyw wayyišlaḥ malʾāḵîm bəʾāšēr ûḇizḇûlun ûḇənaptālî wayyaʿălû liqrāʾtām — 'He sent messengers throughout all Manasseh and they were called out after him; also he sent messengers to Asher, Zebulun, and Naphtali, and they went up to meet them.' The mustering pattern is the šôpār call (v34) followed by messengers (malʾāḵîm H4397) sent outward through the tribes — the same pattern as Saul's muster (1 Sam 11:7). Gideon begins with his own clan (Abiezer, Manasseh) and extends to the northern tribes that had suffered most under Midian's locusts. The willingness of distant tribes to respond to a virtually unknown Gideon testifies to the Spirit's empowerment: the mustering is not merely military but pneumatological.
37: hinnēh ʾānōkî maṣṣîg ʾet-gizzat haṣṣemer baggōren ʾim-ṭal yihyeh ʿal-haggizzāh ləḇaddāh wəʿal-kol-hāʾāreṣ ḥōreḇ — 'Behold, I am placing a fleece of wool on the threshing floor; if there is dew only on the fleece and it is dry on all the ground, then I shall know that you will save Israel by my hand, as you have spoken.' The threshing floor (gōren H1637) is already an important site: Gideon was threshing wheat in a winepress (6:11) to hide from Midian; the threshing floor is the site of harvest vulnerability and covenant celebration (Ruth 3; Hos 9:1). The sign requested operates in the agricultural domain — the very domain Midian has been destroying. YHWH's control over dew (ṭal H2919, the nightly moisture that is gift and blessing in the arid land) confirms his sovereignty over the harvest economy.
38: wayhî-ḵēn wayyaškēm mimmāḥărāt wayyāzeṣ ʾet-haggizzāh wayyiḵeṣ ṭal min-haggizzāh mālôʾ hassəpel mayim — 'And it was so. When he rose early next morning and squeezed the fleece, he wrung dew from the fleece, a bowlful of water.' māzāh (H4330, to wring out/squeeze) — the physical action confirms the miraculous: so much dew in the fleece that it can be wrung out into a bowl. The bowl of water from a single fleece on a dry night is unambiguous. The sign is generous — not a marginal difference but an abundant overflow. YHWH's confirmation is never stingy.
39: wayyōʾmer giḏʿôn ʾel-hāʾĕlōhîm ʾal-yiḥar ʾappəkā bî waʾăḏabbərāh ʾaḵ-happaʿam — 'Gideon said to God: Let not your anger burn against me; let me speak just once more.' ʾal-yiḥar ʾappəkā (let not your anger burn/glow) — the phrase acknowledges the presumption of asking twice; Gideon is aware he is pushing the limits of divine patience. The politeness formula positions him as supplicant, not demander. Yet the second request is the opposite sign: now the fleece dry and the ground wet. The reversal-sign is epistemically stronger — it eliminates the possibility that the fleece merely absorbed ambient moisture. Gideon's second request, though cautious, is honored without rebuke.
